perturbation.original_text: The charge for the services set forth in Article 11.1.1 and 11.1.2 is set forth in the appropriate Support Services Agreement and shall be paid by the Operating Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The charge for the services set forth in Article 11.1.1 and 11.1.2 is set forth in the appropriate Support Services Agreement and the Operating Company will endeavor to pay for these services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be paid' to 'will endeavor to pay' creates ambiguity about the obligation to pay for services rendered. This introduces uncertainty regarding whether the Operating Company is legally bound to fulfill its payment obligations as outlined in the Support Services Agreement, which could lead to disputes and non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law, contracts must be performed in good faith. By introducing the phrase 'will endeavor to pay,' the contract suggests that the Operating Company might not fully commit to fulfilling its financial obligations, which can constitute a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if it's interpreted as a lack of commitment to the terms outlined in the Support Services Agreement. The added phrase makes the obligation of payment less definite, which contradicts the basic principles of contract law requiring clear and definite terms.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be paid' to 'will endeavor to pay' introduces ambiguity about the Operating Company's obligation to pay for services, creating a legal contradiction. The original text creates a clear, enforceable obligation. The modified text weakens this obligation, suggesting a lack of commitment. Snippet 1, under Title 6 Â§ 2-301, states 'The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract.' By creating ambiguity around the payment obligation, the modified text contradicts this general obligation, making the contract less enforceable, because 'endeavor to pay' is not the same as 'accept and pay,' which the law requires.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall produce and submit to the Board, as promptly as possible, a reasonable estimate of their respective income tax liability with respect to the income of the Operating Company computed pursuant to the provisions of this paragraph as by a reasonably prudent taxpayer attempting to reduce the income tax payable by such taxpayer to the greatest extent permitted by law (a "Party Tax Estimate").
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will attempt to produce and submit to the Board an estimate of their income tax liability regarding the income of the Operating Company. This estimate will be computed using methods each party considers appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandated that each party produce a tax estimate as per a 'reasonably prudent taxpayer,' thus defining an objective standard. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that each party 'will attempt' to produce an estimate and gives them full discretion in selecting the calculation methods, replacing the original objective measure with a subjective one. This creates uncertainty and could lead to inconsistent or potentially inaccurate tax estimates.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6662 - Imposition of accuracy-related penalty on underpayments
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows parties to use calculation methods they 'consider appropriate,' which may lead to aggressive tax positions that result in underpayment of taxes. 26 U.S. Code § 6662 imposes penalties for underpayments of tax if there is a substantial understatement of income tax or negligence or disregard of rules or regulations. The ambiguous wording weakens the standard for calculating tax liability, increasing the risk of underpayment and subsequent penalties under federal tax law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6662 - Imposition of accuracy-related penalty on underpayments | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6662 - Imposition of accuracy-related penalty on underpayments U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Imposition of penalty If this section applies to any portion of an underpayment of tax required to be shown on a return, there shall be added to the tax an amount equal to 20 percent of the portion of the underpayment to which this section applies. (b) Portion of underpayment to which section applies This section shall apply to the portion of any underpayment which is attributable to 1 or more of the following: (1) Negligence or disregard of rules or regulations. (2) Any substantial understatement of income tax. (3) Any substantial valuation misstatement under chapter 1. (4) Any substantial overstatement of pension liabilities. (5) Any substantial estate or gift tax valuation understatement . (6) Any disallowance of claimed tax benefits by reason of a transaction lacking economic substance (within the meaning of section 7701(o) ) or failing to meet the requirements of any similar rule of law. (7) Any undisclosed foreign financial asset understatement . (8) Any inconsistent estate basis . (9) Any overstatement of the deduction provided in section 170(p). (10) Any disallowance of a deduction by reason of section 170(h)(7). This section shall not apply to any portion of an underpayment on which a penalty is imposed under section 6663. Except as provided in paragraph (1) or (2)(B) of section 6662A(e), this section shall not apply to the portion of any underpayment which is attributable to a reportable transaction understatement on which a penalty is imposed under section 6662A. (c) Negligence For purposes of this section, the term “ negligence ” includes any failure to make a reasonable attempt to comply with the provisions of this title, a
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 6662 by creating ambiguity that could lead to underpayment of taxes without penalty. The original text established a standard of 'reasonably prudent taxpayer,' whereas the modified version grants each party discretion in choosing calculation methods. This introduces ambiguity, increasing the risk of underpayment. § 6662 imposes penalties for underpayments of tax attributable to negligence or disregard of rules or regulations. The changed text weakens the standard for calculating tax liability, which increases the risk of underpayment and subsequent penalties under federal tax law as stated in the Law Explanation and as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 2: '26 U.S. Code § 6662 - Imposition of accuracy-related penalty on underpayments... (b) Portion of underpayment to which section applies This section shall apply to the portion of any underpayment which is attributable to 1 or more of the following: (1) Negligence or disregard of rules or regulations.'

perturbation.original_text: From and after the date hereof, each Party will use its reasonable best efforts to ensure that (i) no liens or security interest shall be created or levied on the Transferred Assets (except liens for taxes and materialsmen's liens being contested in good faith which are not material in amount), and (ii) the assets to be transferred to the Operating Company, shall be operated in the ordinary course of business consistent with past business practices, and no portion thereof (except inventory sold in the ordinary course of business) shall be sold, conveyed, or otherwise transferred prior to transfer to the Operating Company.
perturbation.changed_text: From and after the date hereof, each Party will endeavor to take steps to ensure that (i) no liens or security interest are created on the Transferred Assets, and (ii) the assets to be transferred to the Operating Company, are operated in a generally acceptable manner.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses the phrase 'reasonable best efforts' which is a defined legal standard. Replacing this with 'endeavor to take steps' weakens the obligation, making it less certain that parties will diligently prevent liens or security interests. Replacing 'ordinary course of business consistent with past business practices' with 'generally acceptable manner' introduces vagueness, as 'generally acceptable' is subjective and undefined, potentially leading to disputes over how the assets should be managed. The original text was clear on maintaining established practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Care/Breach of Fiduciary Duty
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law (where the contract is governed) requires parties to act with a duty of care. By replacing the stricter 'reasonable best efforts' with the looser 'endeavor to take steps,' and by replacing 'ordinary course of business' with 'generally acceptable manner,' the revised text weakens the standard of care expected. This increases the risk that the parties could fail to protect the Transferred Assets adequately, potentially leading to a breach of fiduciary duty if such failure results in harm to the Joint Venture. The ambiguous wording undermines the requirement that parties act diligently and responsibly in managing assets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the legal obligation by replacing 'reasonable best efforts' with 'endeavor to take steps' and 'ordinary course of business consistent with past business practices' with 'generally acceptable manner.' This introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses the powers and duties of a board of directors, implying a responsibility to manage the corporation's affairs diligently. The original contract language provided a clearer, more enforceable standard, while the changed language opens the door for disputes over what is 'generally acceptable,' making it difficult to enforce the agreement and potentially leading to a breach of fiduciary duty by weakening the standard of care expected of the parties, therefore creating a contradiction.
